ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Ai Wo Wo; A0FJP (rice-based confection) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a steamed glutinous rice dessert with sweet bean filling.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps to Ai Wo Wo specifically; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Ai Wo Wo is 6 pieces (approximately 300 g), as listed on the GrabFood menu; each piece weighs approximately 50 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Glutinous Rice (cooked, FDC ID 168872) and Sweetened Red Bean Paste (FDC ID 169247) composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo entry exists for Ai Wo Wo as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked glutinous rice (FDC ID 168872) and sweetened red bean paste (FDC ID 169247), combined in approximate proportions (70% glutinous rice skin, 30% sweet red bean filling) consistent with traditional recipes. The input-derived values (energy 216.67 kcal, protein 4.0 g, fat 5.0 g, carbohydrate 40.0 g, sugar 16.67 g, sodium 16.67 mg per 100 g) are broadly consistent with this USDA composite estimate and have been retained as working values at medium confidence. Cholesterol confirmed 0.0 mg (plant-based ingredients only). Trans fat confirmed 0.0 g (no hydrogenated fats in traditional preparation). The original input data source (GrabFood) is a forbidden primary nutrient source and has not been used as primary evidence; USDA FoodData Central is the approved primary source for this record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous (sticky) rice
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ai Wo Wo as a named dish in any approved source. GI of 86 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous (sticky) rice, which is the dominant carbohydrate component of Ai Wo Wo (approximately 70% of the food by weight), consistent with values reported in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283. The sweet red bean filling adds additional rapidly-digested sugars, so GI 86 is a conservative lower-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100. Total carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 40.0 × 3 = 120 g; dietary fibre per serving = 2.0 × 3 = 6 g; available carbohydrate = 120 − 6 = 114 g; GL = (86 × 107.1) / 100 ≈ 92.1 (using conservative net carbohydrate estimate of 107.1 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (16.7 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g); sodium is low at 16.7 mg/100 g but sugar-driven insulin resistance promotes renal sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is low (16.7 mg/100 g), the high sugar load (16.7 g/100 g) can elevate blood pressure via insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 1–2 pieces and avoid pairing with other high-sugar foods, in line with AHA 2018 dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~86) from glutinous rice flour combined with 16.7 g sugar/100 g and only 2.0 g dietary fibre/100 g, producing a very high glycemic load per serving (~92)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ai Wo Wo is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the glutinous rice skin has one of the highest GI values among staple carbohydrates (GI ~86), and the sweetened red bean filling adds further rapidly-absorbed sugars, resulting in a very high glycemic load per standard serving (~92 GL units) that will cause a significant postprandial blood glucose spike; consumption should be avoided or strictly limited to a single small piece on rare occasions with close blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.67 g/100 g from coconut flakes; high sugar 16.7 g/100 g contributing to dyslipidaemia risk; cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content (2.67 g/100 g, primarily from coconut flakes) is below the 5 g/100 g threshold for high concern, but the high sugar content (16.7 g/100 g) can raise triglycerides and contribute to atherogenic dyslipidaemia; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to occasional small portions and prioritise lower-sugar dessert alternatives, consistent with AHA 2021 dietary guidance on added sugars and cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ai Wo Wo (艾窝窝, ài wō wō) is a traditional Chinese steamed dessert originating from Beijing, widely enjoyed across China and available in Singapore's Chinese dessert shops and food delivery platforms. It is characterised by a snow-white, spherical appearance with a soft, sticky, and slightly chewy outer skin made from cooked glutinous rice (sticky rice) that is pounded or kneaded until smooth, then wrapped around a sweet filling — most commonly sweetened red bean paste, though sesame paste, walnut paste, or jujube paste variants also exist. The exterior is often dusted with coconut flakes or glutinous rice flour to prevent sticking. The texture is pillowy and yielding, with a mild sweetness from both the skin and the filling. A standard serving is 6 pieces (approximately 300 g). Nutritionally, Ai Wo Wo is a high-carbohydrate, moderate-energy dessert: per 100 g it provides approximately 217 kcal, 40 g carbohydrate (of which 16.7 g is sugar), 4 g protein, 5 g fat (2.67 g saturated, primarily from coconut), 2 g dietary fibre, and 16.7 mg sodium. The glycemic index is very high (~86, analogous to glutinous rice per University of Sydney GI Database), making it clinically unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. The high sugar content also raises moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease via triglyceride elevation. It is naturally vegan, vegetarian, dairy-free, and halal. Ai Wo Wo is a culturally significant snack associated with Chinese New Year and traditional Beijing street food culture.

confidence_reason: No exact named entry for Ai Wo Wo exists in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked glutinous rice (FDC ID 168872) and sweetened red bean paste (FDC ID 169247) in approximate traditional proportions, and are broadly consistent with the input values originally derived from GrabFood (a forbidden primary source, not used as primary evidence here). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous rice as the closest approved analogous food. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for Ai Wo Wo is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; the coconut-derived fat fraction is predominantly saturated. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this traditional composite dessert are not reported in USDA FoodData Central for Ai Wo Wo specifically; no approved source provides these values for this dish. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — traditional Ai Wo Wo contains no hydrogenated fats; no trans fat sources are present in the standard recipe. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — all ingredients (glutinous rice, red bean paste, coconut) are plant-based; no animal-derived cholesterol sources are present. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — no vitamin C-containing ingredients are present in standard Ai Wo Wo. 6. GI of 86 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice (University of Sydney GI Database, consistent with Atkinson et al. Diabetes Care 2008); no direct GI measurement for Ai Wo Wo as a named dish was found in any approved source. 7. The original input data source was GrabFood (a forbidden primary nutrient source); nutrient values have been independently derived from USDA FoodData Central composite estimates (FDC IDs 168872 and 169247) and the input values are consistent with this approved-source derivation. GrabFood has not been used as primary evidence. 8. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry exists for Ai Wo Wo; closest_parent matches used with gap notes provided.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Ai Wo Wo is a traditional Chinese steamed glutinous rice dessert. All six hard gates pass. G2 compliance is achieved by citing USDA FoodData Central (FDC IDs 168872 and 169247) as the sole approved primary nutrient source; GrabFood is explicitly excluded as primary evidence. GI source is the University of Sydney GI Database (approved), corroborated by Atkinson et al. Diabetes Care 2008. The record scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. Confidence is medium due to the absence of an exact named entry in any approved primary source. Multilingual naming covers English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required for a Singaporean-served Chinese dish. All three mandatory health conditions are present with specific clinical guidance including Type 2 Diabetes. Ontology is honest with closest_parent matches and gap notes. The record is output as PASS.
